writing_task: Hey, can you help me draft a quick email to our lead engineer letting them know they're the single owner on the bug fix — deadline is EOD Friday? Want to make sure the tone lands right: direct but not pressure-y, and no fluff.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Bug fix ownership — EOD Friday

Hey [Name],

You're the sole owner on this bug fix, with a hard deadline of EOD Friday. I know that's a tight window and may shift things you already have lined up — that's not lost on me.

If anything comes up that makes Friday unrealistic, flag it today so we can figure out the path forward before it becomes a problem.

[Your name]
drafts.generic:
Subject: Bug Fix Ownership – EOD Friday Deadline

Hi [Name],

Just wanted to confirm you're the sole owner on the bug fix we discussed. That means decisions, progress, and delivery are all yours to drive — I trust you've got it.

Deadline is end of day Friday. If anything comes up that might affect that timeline, just flag it early so we can sort it out together.

Let me know if you need anything from me in the meantime.

Thanks,
[Your Name]
drafts.preference:
Subject: Bug Fix Ownership – EOD Friday Deadline

Hi [Name],

You're the sole owner on the bug fix, so all decisions and delivery are on you. Deadline is EOD Friday.

If you hit blockers or need anything from my end before then, flag it early — don't wait until Friday afternoon.

Can you confirm you're good to own this and on track to hit the deadline?

Thanks,
[Your Name]

user_reason: B is the only one that frames the stakes from the engineer's perspective — acknowledging their existing workload upfront before delivering the ownership/deadline news. The closing is action-oriented without being demanding. A is solid and direct but feels slightly pressure-y with the confirmation ask, and the order is a bit abrupt — news before any acknowledgment. C is the weakest: 'I trust you've got it' reads as filler, 'sort it out together' undercuts the single-owner framing, and 'let me know if you need anything' after the CTA is trailing sentiment that weakens the close.

suggested_rubric.name: Directness and Conciseness
suggested_rubric.description: Every sentence must earn its place. No fluff, no padding, no process language disguised as substance. The message should get to the point quickly and stay there, reading like a team email rather than a project document.

dimensions.label: No sentences that exist solely for politeness, transition, or padding without carrying new information
The core message (what changed, what's happening) appears within the first few lines
The format matches the weight of the message — avoids excessive bullet points, headers, or project-plan scaffolding for simple communications
Every structural element (bullets, bold, sections) is present because it clarifies, not because it adds a sense of thoroughness

suggested_rubric.name: Audience-Centric Framing
suggested_rubric.description: The 'why this matters' must land from the reader's side of the table, not the sender's. Reasoning should acknowledge the real-world impact on the audience — their plans, their commitments, their disruptions — before or instead of explaining the sender's decision-making process.

dimensions.label: The message names the reader's real-world disruptions or concerns before explaining the sender's rationale
The message does not substitute the sender's reasoning (e.g., 'we want to be confident') for genuine acknowledgment of the reader's situation
Acknowledgment references concrete reader-side effects (e.g., disrupted plans, commitments to others) rather than abstract empathy
Any request or action item comes after the reader's reality has been addressed, not before

suggested_rubric.name: Actionable Closure
suggested_rubric.description: The message must end on forward momentum — something that moves people toward the next concrete thing, not something that consoles, schedules a vague check-in, or leaves the reader sitting with unresolved emotion. The landing point should give people something to do with the next 24 hours.

dimensions.label: The final lines point forward to a concrete action or update, not to a feeling or reassurance
Closing does not propose meetings or check-ins as substitutes for clear next steps
The reader can identify what specifically happens next after reading the last line
The message does not end on a consoling or morale-boosting note that replaces a concrete landing point

suggested_rubric.name: Focused Accountability
suggested_rubric.description: Action items should name one person accountable with one deadline. Multiple owners, parallel asks, and group-diffused responsibility read as noise. Minimal and clear beats thorough and comprehensive.

dimensions.label: One specific person is named as accountable for the primary action, not a team or group
One concrete deadline is attached to the primary action
The message avoids stacking multiple unrelated asks or action items that compete for attention

suggested_rubric.name: Tone Calibration
suggested_rubric.description: Professional and grounded without being cold, performative, or pep-talky. Acknowledgment should be genuine and brief — phrases like 'That's not lost on me' over elaborate apologies. The message should build trust through straightforwardness, not through emotional signaling.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of difficulty is brief and genuine, not over-apologetic or theatrical
The message avoids rah-rah or morale-boosting language that substitutes for substance
The message signals honesty through plain, straight language rather than through explicit claims of transparency
The tone registers as human and respectful without tipping into casual or consoling
The message avoids imperative or warning phrasing that implies the reader needs to be told how to behave (e.g., 'don't wait until Friday afternoon') — pressure lands through clarity, not instruction

writing_task: Hey, can you help me draft a quick email to a vendor letting them know we're pushing the contract renewal date by two weeks? They were expecting to hear from us by end of this month, so I want to acknowledge that upfront before getting into it.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Contract Renewal Timeline Update

Hi [Name],

We'd been planning to get back to you by end of month, so I want to flag this before that date arrives: we need to push the renewal decision by two weeks, to [new date]. I know that affects your planning on your end, particularly if you've been holding resources or commitments in anticipation of this.

We'll have a final answer to you by [new date] — no further delays expected.

Thanks for bearing with us.

[Your name]
drafts.generic:
Subject: Contract Renewal Timeline Update

Hi [Vendor Name],

I wanted to reach out before the end of the month, as we had originally indicated you'd hear from us by then. We appreciate your patience and wanted to give you a heads-up that we're pushing our contract renewal decision back by two weeks. This is due to some internal scheduling on our end and is in no way a reflection of our experience working with you.

We expect to be back in touch by [new date]. Please let me know if this creates any issues on your side.

Thanks,
[Your Name]
drafts.preference:
Subject: Contract Renewal Timeline Update

Hi [Vendor Name],

I wanted to give you a heads-up before the end of the month — we're pushing our contract renewal decision back by two weeks. I know you were expecting to hear from us soon, and I appreciate your patience as we work through some internal sign-offs.

Nothing has changed on our end in terms of intent; we just need a bit more time to finalize things on our side.

Could you confirm that the updated timeline works for your team? Happy to jump on a quick call if that's easier.

Thanks,
[Your Name]

user_reason: B is the closest to what I'd actually send. It opens by acknowledging the original expectation, delivers the news with a concrete date, and frames the delay from the vendor's perspective — 'affects your planning, particularly if you've been holding resources' is exactly the right framing. The one issue is 'Thanks for bearing with us' at the end, which is trailing sentiment after the CTA and weakens it. A is decent — the structure is mostly right and it has a clear ask — but 'I appreciate your patience as we work through some internal sign-offs' centers our internal process rather than their situation, and 'Happy to jump on a quick call if that's easier' is a bit soft as a CTA. C is the weakest by a lot. It over-explains, reassures them it's 'not a reflection of our experience working with you' which nobody asked and reads as filler, and the closing is passive. No clear owner, no real deadline commitment, just 'let me know if this creates any issues.'

criteria_analysis.name: Information Sequencing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails the sequencing the user has repeatedly asked for: it opens with a brief orienting beat ('We'd been planning to get back to you by end of month, so I want to flag this before that date arrives'), then delivers the news, then layers in impact — no front-loading of context. The generic draft front-loads appreciation and an explanation ('due to some internal scheduling') before the reader fully absorbs the news, creating that 'prepped for bad news' feeling. The original preference draft is solid — orients briefly then delivers — but layers in sender-side reasoning ('work through some internal sign-offs') before fully grounding impact.

criteria_analysis.name: Audience-Centric Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly names what's at stake for the vendor ('affects your planning on your end, particularly if you've been holding resources or commitments') — concrete, reader-world language. The generic draft substitutes sender reasoning ('internal scheduling on our end') and a defensive reassurance ('in no way a reflection of our experience') as empathy, which is exactly the pattern the user rejects. The original preference draft acknowledges the vendor was expecting to hear back but frames impact abstractly ('intent hasn't changed,' 'finalize things on our side') — sender-side language, not reader-stakes language.

criteria_analysis.name: Focused Accountability
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names one clear commitment (final answer by [new date]) and adds 'no further delays expected,' which reinforces singular accountability on the sender without diffusing ownership. The generic draft is clean — one deadline, one expectation — though 'let me know if this creates any issues' slightly opens a parallel thread. The original preference draft introduces a competing ask ('Could you confirm the updated timeline works?') and offers a call, which diffuses the clear next step and splits the reader's attention.

criteria_analysis.name: Actionable Closure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft closes on a concrete commitment ('We'll have a final answer to you by [new date] — no further delays expected'), giving the reader something to expect. The 'Thanks for bearing with us' is a mild trailing note but doesn't undermine the forward momentum. The generic draft's closing ('Please let me know if this creates any issues') is vaguely actionable but reactive rather than forward-moving. The original preference draft closes by proposing a call and asking for confirmation — a placeholder check-in that substitutes for a clear next step, which the rubric explicitly flags.

criteria_analysis.name: Directness and Conciseness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is tight — every sentence carries new information, the core message lands in the second line, and there's no padding. The generic draft includes filler ('We appreciate your patience,' 'in no way a reflection of our experience') that adds no informational value. suggested_rubric.name: Information Sequencing
suggested_rubric.description: The order in which information lands matters as much as the content. The reader should be briefly oriented before receiving news or responsibility, the core message should be stated before supporting reasoning is layered in, and context should never front-load before the reader knows what the message is about.

dimensions.label: A brief beat orients the reader or acknowledges their context before the core message or responsibility is stated — the news doesn't land cold
The main news or ask is stated before supporting reasoning, stakes, or justification is provided
Context and stakes are not front-loaded before the reader knows what the message is about — no feeling of being 'prepped for bad news'
Information builds in layers where each sentence prepares the reader for what follows, creating a conversational flow rather than a directive

suggested_rubric.name: Audience-Centric Framing
suggested_rubric.description: The 'why this matters' must land from the reader's side of the table. Reasoning should name the concrete stakes and real-world impact on the reader's work — their plans, commitments, and blocked dependencies — rather than expressing the sender's feelings about the situation or explaining the sender's decision-making process.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment references what's at stake for the reader's work or team, not the sender's emotional awareness of the difficulty
Impact language names specific real-world effects on the reader (disrupted plans, broken commitments, blocked work) rather than abstract organizational concepts
The message does not substitute the sender's rationale or confidence language for genuine acknowledgment of the reader's situation
Language is grounded in the reader's actual day-to-day reality rather than process-level abstractions like 'downstream dependencies'

suggested_rubric.name: Focused Accountability
suggested_rubric.description: Action items should name one person accountable with one deadline. Multiple owners, parallel asks, and group-diffused responsibility read as noise and make the email feel like a project plan rather than a direct communication.

dimensions.label: One specific person is named as accountable for the primary action, not a team or group
One concrete deadline is attached to the primary action
The message avoids stacking multiple unrelated asks or action items that compete for attention
Ownership established for one person is not immediately diluted by shifting focus back to a group or scheduling a group check-in

suggested_rubric.name: Actionable Closure
suggested_rubric.description: The message must end on forward momentum — something that moves people toward the next concrete thing. The landing point should give the reader something to do or expect, not console them, schedule a vague check-in, or leave them sitting with unresolved emotion.

dimensions.label: The final lines point forward to a concrete action or update, not to a feeling or reassurance
Closing does not propose meetings or check-ins as substitutes for clear next steps
The reader can identify what specifically happens next after reading the last line
The message does not end on a consoling or morale-boosting note that replaces a concrete landing point
No warm sign-off, gratitude phrase, or sentiment appears after the forward-momentum statement — the concrete next step or commitment is the last thing the reader sees

suggested_rubric.name: Directness and Conciseness
suggested_rubric.description: Every sentence must earn its place. No fluff, no padding, no process language disguised as substance. The message should get to the point quickly and stay there, reading like a team email rather than a project document.

dimensions.label: No sentences that exist solely for politeness, transition, or padding without carrying new information
The core message (what changed, what's being asked) appears within the first few lines
The format matches the weight of the message — avoids excessive bullet points, headers, or project-plan scaffolding for simple communications
Every structural element (bullets, bold, sections) is present because it clarifies, not because it adds a sense of thoroughness

suggested_rubric.name: Tone Calibration
suggested_rubric.description: Professional and grounded without being cold, performative, or pressure-laden. Acknowledgment should be brief and stake-oriented rather than emotionally self-referential. The message should feel like a conversation between colleagues, not a directive from above or a sympathy note.

dimensions.label: Acknowledgment of difficulty is brief and grounded in stakes, not over-apologetic, theatrical, or centered on the sender's feelings
The message avoids rah-rah or morale-boosting language that substitutes for substance
The message signals honesty through plain, straight language rather than through explicit claims of transparency
The tone registers as human and respectful — feels like a conversation rather than a directive — without tipping into casual or consoling
Urgency and accountability land through clarity of stakes and deadlines, not through imperative language, warnings, or implied pressure

writing_task: Hey, I need to draft another client email — this time it's a scope change conversation. We agreed on X, they've been building plans around it, and now we need to add something that'll affect the timeline. Can you help me write something that doesn't come across as we're just dumping more on them?

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Scope update — affects the timeline we've been working from

Hi [Name],

You've had plans in motion based on what we agreed, so I want to flag this directly: we need to add [brief description of new element], and it's going to push the current timeline by [X weeks/days].

The reason it can't wait is [one-sentence explanation of why this needs to happen now — tied to something concrete, not internal politics].

I'd ask [specific person] to come back to us by [specific date] with a revised schedule reflecting the change. That'll give your team a clean picture to work from before anything downstream gets locked in.

[Your name]
drafts.generic:
Subject: Update on Project Scope — Quick Chat Needed

Hi [Name],

I wanted to reach out before this affects your planning any further. As we've been moving forward, it's become clear that [new addition] needs to be incorporated to ensure the overall outcome holds up the way we both want it to.

I know you've already built timelines around what we agreed on, so I don't take this lightly. The honest reality is this adds roughly [X time] to our schedule. I'd love to walk through it together so we can adjust in a way that works for everyone.

Can we find 20 minutes this week?

[Your Name]
drafts.preference:
Subject: Scope Update — Timeline Impact

Hi [Name],

I want to get ahead of something before it catches you off guard. We need to add [scope item] to the project, and it will push the timeline by approximately [X weeks].

I know you've been building plans around the original schedule, so I didn't want to sit on this. The addition is necessary because [brief reason], and addressing it now keeps us from running into bigger issues later.

Can we get 20 minutes on the calendar this week to walk through the adjustment together and realign on next steps?

Thanks,
[Your name]

user_reason: A is the only one that gets the order right — it acknowledges their situation first, delivers the news cleanly, explains why it matters from their perspective (downstream plans getting locked in), and ends with a single named owner and deadline. No trailing sentiment. C is decent but 'keeps us from running into bigger issues later' is sender-framed reasoning, and 'Thanks' after the CTA is exactly the kind of trailing sentiment that weakens the close. B is the weakest — 'I'd love to walk through it together' and 'works for everyone' are soft and slightly jargon-adjacent, 'ensure the overall outcome holds up the way we both want it to' buries the news, and the whole thing feels like it's managing our discomfort more than respecting their time.
